options.C: The sensitivity and specificity of the model at the optimal cut-off
options.D: The reference category must always be the intervention group, not the control group

explanation.correct: SAMPL guidelines for logistic regression reporting require: the regression coefficients or odds ratios with 95% CIs for each variable; the model's overall fit (Hosmer-Lemeshow test p-value); and the model's discriminatory ability (c-statistic/AUC). Reporting individual predictor ORs without model-level performance metrics is incomplete — a model can have several individually significant predictors yet perform poorly overall (low AUC) due to collinearity or model misspecification. The Hosmer-Lemeshow test assesses calibration (whether predicted probabilities match observed event rates); the c-statistic assesses discrimination (how well the model separates cases from non-cases).

wrong.A: This is the correct answer, not a wrong answer — c-statistic and Hosmer-Lemeshow are the key missing elements.
wrong.B: Standardised beta coefficients are used in linear regression for continuous outcomes — not appropriate for logistic regression where the OR already provides the measure of association.
wrong.C: Sensitivity and specificity at a cut-off are relevant for a diagnostic prediction model but not required for a regression analysis of risk factors.
wrong.D: The reference category convention (which group is the comparator) is a display choice — it does not affect SAMPL compliance.

explanation.guideline: SAMPL Guidelines 2015 — logistic regression: 'Report odds ratios (with 95% CIs), the overall model fit (e.g., Hosmer-Lemeshow test), and the model's discriminatory ability (e.g., c-statistic)'
explanation.trial: Harrell FE. Regression Modeling Strategies. Springer 2001 — definitive reference for regression reporting standards.
explanation.pearl: Multivariable logistic regression minimum reporting set: individual predictor ORs + 95%CIs + p-values; model AUC/c-statistic; Hosmer-Lemeshow p-value; number of events per variable (EPV ≥10 recommended). Missing any element will trigger a reviewer revision request.

questions.stem: A surgical team creates a visual abstract for a JAMA Surgery submission showing the study's Kaplan-Meier survival result. The visual abstract uses a red/green colour scheme to distinguish the two patient groups. A reviewer raises a concern about accessibility. What is the issue?

options.A: Visual abstracts are not permitted by JAMA Surgery
options.B: Red-green colour combinations are inaccessible to individuals with red-green colour blindness (deuteranopia/protanopia), affecting approximately 8% of males
options.C: The visual abstract should use black and white only to ensure print compatibility
options.D: Kaplan-Meier curves cannot be displayed in visual abstracts due to information density

explanation.correct: Red-green colour blindness (deuteranopia and protanopia) affects approximately 8% of males and 0.5% of females. Using red and green as the only distinguishing feature between comparison groups makes the figure inaccessible to this population — they cannot distinguish the two survival curves. Journal guidelines and universal design principles require colorblind-safe palettes. Recommended alternatives: blue-orange (excellent contrast for all colour vision types); blue-red (better than red-green for most colour vision deficiencies); or supplementing colour with line style differences (solid vs dashed) so that the groups are distinguishable even in greyscale or for colour-blind readers.

wrong.A: JAMA Surgery actively encourages visual abstracts and provides specific formatting guidelines for them.
wrong.C: Black and white is not required — colourblind-safe colour palettes (blue-orange, blue-red) are preferred over greyscale.
wrong.D: Kaplan-Meier curves are among the most commonly used figure types in visual abstracts for survival studies.

explanation.guideline: JAMA Surgery author guidelines — figures should use colourblind-safe palettes; EQUATOR visual abstract standards recommend blue-orange colour scheme
explanation.trial: Wong B. Points of view: Color blindness. Nature Methods 2011;8:441 — foundational guidance on colorblind-safe figure design.
explanation.pearl: Colorblind-safe colour pairs: blue + orange (best); blue + red; purple + orange. Test any figure using Coblis (Color Blindness Simulator) or the Ishihara simulation tool before submission. Always add line-style differentiation as a secondary distinguishing feature.

questions.stem: An author uses AI writing assistance to draft the Discussion section of a surgical manuscript. The final Discussion has been substantially revised and critically reviewed by all authors. What is the correct disclosure practice according to current ICMJE and major journal policies?

options.A: AI tools cannot be listed as authors — all writing produced by AI must be disclosed in the Methods or Acknowledgements section with description of the tool used
options.B: AI assistance in writing does not require disclosure as long as the content has been verified by human authors
options.C: AI tools should be listed as co-authors to ensure accountability for AI-generated content
options.D: Disclosure is only required if the AI generated the primary statistical analysis, not the writing

explanation.correct: ICMJE (2023) and virtually all major journals (NEJM, Lancet, Nature, BJS, EJSO) now require disclosure of AI tool use in manuscript preparation. AI tools cannot be listed as authors — they cannot meet ICMJE authorship criteria (particularly criterion 4: accountability for the work). Use must be disclosed in the Methods section (if used for data analysis) or Acknowledgements/cover letter (if used for writing assistance), specifying the tool name and version. The rationale: readers and reviewers have a right to know how the manuscript was prepared; AI tools can hallucinate references, misrepresent findings, or introduce subtle errors that human review may not catch.

wrong.A: This is the correct answer — AI tools cannot be authors and use must be disclosed.
wrong.B: Human verification does not eliminate the disclosure obligation — the tool's contribution must be transparent regardless of subsequent human review.
wrong.C: AI tools do not meet ICMJE authorship criteria and cannot be held accountable for integrity concerns — they cannot be listed as authors under any circumstances.
wrong.D: Disclosure applies to any use of AI in manuscript preparation, including writing, editing, or figure generation — not limited to statistical analysis.

explanation.guideline: ICMJE 2023 update — AI tools may not be listed as authors; their use in manuscript preparation must be disclosed; corresponding author takes responsibility for AI-generated content
explanation.trial: Thacker SB. Meta-analysis: a quantitative approach to research integration. JAMA 1988 — predates AI, but the principle of transparent methodology reporting is foundational.
explanation.pearl: Standard AI disclosure language (adapt as needed): 'The authors used [Tool Name, Version] (Company, Location) to assist with initial drafting of the Discussion section. The AI-generated text was subsequently reviewed, substantially revised, and critically appraised by all authors, who take full responsibility for the accuracy and integrity of the final manuscript.'
